topics.title: Day 1: Understanding Compensation
topics.content:
> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Mastering Day 1: Understanding Compensation is a must-have for any cybersecurity role. SOC analysts use this daily, penetration testers build on it, and hiring managers specifically look for this skill on resumes.

:::objectives
- Understand security compensation
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Explain Day 1: Understanding Compensation and its role in Salary, negotiation, offers, benefits
topics.interviewQuestion: What should you know about cybersecurity compensation?
topics.interviewAnswer: Components: base salary, bonus (typically 5-20%), equity/RSUs (public companies), sign-on bonus, relocation, training budget, certification bonuses, conference budget. Ranges: SOC L1 (60-85k), Security Engineer (100-140k), Senior Engineer (140-180k), Architect (160-220k), CISO (200k+). Geographic and industry variations matter.

quiz.question: What is typical bonus range?
What is SOC L1 salary range?
What is Security Engineer salary range?
What location pays highest?
What industry pays highest?
Which best describes Day 1: Understanding Compensation?
quiz.explanation: Day 1: Understanding Compensation is a foundational cybersecurity concept that defines how systems work. It is a security principle, not a programming language or protocol.

quiz.question: What is the primary purpose of Day 1: Understanding Compensation?
quiz.explanation: Security principles like those in Day 1: Understanding Compensation typically span confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

quiz.question: Where does Day 1: Understanding Compensation apply in real-world scenarios?
quiz.explanation: Day 1: Understanding Compensation concepts are universal across all security domains.

quiz.question: Which certification exam covers Day 1: Understanding Compensation?
quiz.explanation: Most major certifications touch on Day 1: Understanding Compensation at some level.

quiz.question: Best way to gain hands-on experience with Day 1: Understanding Compensation?
quiz.explanation: Practical application in a home lab is the most effective way to build skills. Reading alone is not enough.

quiz.question: Which skill is NOT directly related to Day 1: Understanding Compensation?
quiz.explanation: While web development may use security concepts, Day 1: Understanding Compensation is more directly tied to risk, response, and monitoring.

quiz.question: Scenario: You encounter Day 1: Understanding Compensation during a security audit. What is your first step?
quiz.explanation: A professional approach to Day 1: Understanding Compensation during an audit is to identify the issue and document findings for further analysis.

quiz.question: What is a common tool used with Day 1: Understanding Compensation?
quiz.explanation: Depending on context, Day 1: Understanding Compensation can involve firewalls, SIEMs, or packet analyzers.

quiz.question: Understanding Day 1: Understanding Compensation helps prevent which type of attack?
quiz.explanation: The specific attack Day 1: Understanding Compensation prevents depends on its context within the security domain.

quiz.question: How does Day 1: Understanding Compensation relate to the CIA triad?
quiz.explanation: Most security concepts including Day 1: Understanding Compensation can impact all three pillars of the CIA triad.

topics.title: Day 2: Negotiation Strategy
topics.content:
> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Mastering Day 2: Negotiation Strategy is a must-have for any cybersecurity role. SOC analysts use this daily, penetration testers build on it, and hiring managers specifically look for this skill on resumes.

:::objectives
- Develop negotiation strategy
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Explain Day 2: Negotiation Strategy and its role in Salary, negotiation, offers, benefits
topics.interviewQuestion: How do you negotiate a cybersecurity job offer?
topics.interviewAnswer: Research: know market rate for role, location, experience (Levels.fyi, Glassdoor, LinkedIn, BLS). Never give number first - let them give range. Negotiate total comp, not just base. Leverage competing offers. Be professional: express enthusiasm, state facts, collaborate on solution. Everything negotiable: salary, bonus, start date, remote days.

quiz.question: What is the golden rule of negotiation?
What tool helps research compensation?
What should you negotiate beyond salary?
options: Total compensation including bonus, equity, training

quiz.question: What strengthens negotiation position?
What is the best negotiation approach?

topics.title: Day 3: Evaluating Job Offers
topics.content:
> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Mastering Day 3: Evaluating Job Offers is a must-have for any cybersecurity role. SOC analysts use this daily, penetration testers build on it, and hiring managers specifically look for this skill on resumes.

:::objectives
- Evaluate job offers
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Explain Day 3: Evaluating Job Offers and its role in Salary, negotiation, offers, benefits
topics.interviewQuestion: What to consider when evaluating security job offers?
topics.interviewAnswer: Beyond comp: growth opportunities (training budget, mentorship, promotion path), team quality, tools and tech stack, on-call requirements, security culture, work-life balance, company stability, industry, mission. Consider total package match with personal priorities. 30-60-90 day plan can show commitment.

quiz.question: What is the most underrated factor?
What indicates company security culture?
What does on-call requirement affect?
What shows candidate commitment?
What should match personal priorities?
options: Total package - comp, growth, culture, balance

topics.title: Day 4: Offer Letter and Contract
topics.content:
> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Mastering Day 4: Offer Letter and Contract is a must-have for any cybersecurity role. SOC analysts use this daily, penetration testers build on it, and hiring managers specifically look for this skill on resumes.

:::objectives
- Review offer terms
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Explain Day 4: Offer Letter and Contract and its role in Salary, negotiation, offers, benefits
topics.interviewQuestion: What should you review in a cybersecurity offer letter?
topics.interviewAnswer: At-will employment, start date, salary, bonus terms, equity/stock, benefits start date, PTO, sick leave, training budget, non-compete and non-solicit agreements (note: some states restrict non-competes), IP assignment. Verify everything in writing. Ask clarification questions before signing.

quiz.question: What employment type is standard?
What should be verified in writing?
What clauses may restrict future jobs?
What does IP assignment cover?
options: Who owns work created during employment

quiz.question: What should you do before signing?
Which best describes Day 4: Offer Letter and Contract?

topics.title: Day 5: Starting a New Role
topics.content:
> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Mastering Day 5: Starting a New Role is a must-have for any cybersecurity role. SOC analysts use this daily, penetration testers build on it, and hiring managers specifically look for this skill on resumes.

:::objectives
- Start a new security role
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Explain Day 5: Starting a New Role and its role in Salary, negotiation, offers, benefits
topics.interviewQuestion: How do you start a new cybersecurity role successfully?
topics.interviewAnswer: First 30 days: learn - meet team, learn tools, understand processes, read documentation. 30-60 days: contribute - take on small tasks, ask questions, build relationships. 60-90 days: own - deliver project, suggest improvements, establish credibility. Document everything. Find a mentor in the org.

quiz.question: What is first 30 days focus?
What is 30-60 day focus?
What is 60-90 day focus?
Why document everything?
Who should you find in new organization?
options: A mentor to guide and advocate
